speaker: interviewee
text: Is it OK now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So let me go now to the labor camp. In the forced labor camp, I met up with one of my uncles, with whom I was together from that point on, which is June 1944 until after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frank Ferenc Friedman, my mother's brother. And in the-- he was extremely religious, even more so than I was, maybe even my father was. And he, unfortunately, had took it upon himself that he ate nothing but kosher in the first six months in the forced labor camp. But in the labor camp-- let me say this, that it was an interesting situation. We were under Hungarian guards, but working for the Germans. We lived-- what they made us to live in, in a stable, in a former-- we had stable. And Hungarian guards took us out to the workplace, which was some four miles from where we slept. And there we were under German supervision and guidance. First day when we arrived with the Hungarians, two German, very lowly ranked soldiers-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --took us over and asked, in German-- and the Hungarians don't speak German, only us Jews spoke with them-- and asked one of our Jewish leaders, who are those guys with the weapons behind us? So they told them, they are our guards. He said, well, tell them that either they should take some tools also and work with us, or get the hell out of here.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we-- when the Jewish guys translated it to the Hungarian lieutenant, he turned around and wanted to kick the Jew in the [LAUGHTER] behind. And the German took out his pistol and he said-- [LAUGHS] pointed it at the lieutenant-- this was a German private first class, maybe-- [LAUGHS] and said, tell him-- tell the Jew-- that if he ever touches you again in front of me, I will shoot him. [LAUGHS] So anyhow, this is how-- and I have to say that these Germans were not SS. They were the Wehrmacht, the regular army Germans, who did not know about these stories about the Jews have to be handled like animals. So from that point on, really, the Hungarians were taking us out, taking back, but they gave it back to us in our own circle, of course. So anyhow, they were asking for different type of treatment, the Germans. The [? Schneiders-- ?] these tailors, they went into the German headquarters. They worked there. They had a good job. They asked for clerks. There were a couple of news reporters, so they became the Germans' clerks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they asked for gardeners, so that was tailor-made for me. So I became their gardener. As a matter of fact, I took my uncle with me and a couple other guys. So for that period of six months while I was there, I had a lot of very good times in there. I had food. We were able to being back with my uncle from the Germans raw potatoes, flour that my uncle, [NON-ENGLISH], set up a kosher kitchen. There were six or seven of them. We ate nothing but kosher, including me in the beginning, too. And so this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of gardening did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around them, in their headquarters-- this was from June until November-- I was planting flowers, cutting rose bushes. They had some fruit trees I took care of. So anyhow, it was a regular job from morning till evening for me. One interesting story, but I have to go back for a while. When I was-- right after bar mitzvah, in Gyongyos I remember they were reading the Torah. And after, you know, they finished, an old man, the shamash, was taking down the Torah, and there were four steps. And he stumbled, fell, and the Torah fell out of his hand. And not only that, but the cover of the Torah, the cloth, was pulled off, and the Torah, naked, lay there on the floor. And that was a terrible tragedy. The-- --community, they divided 40 people. Each had to fast for one day because of that fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while I was in this camp, I became sick. I got a cold and I developed a pneumonia. And I was in the stable lying there in high fever, and the Germans were looking for me. Where is the little gardener, they called me. So-- and they came into the stable and found me there, shivering and burning up with fever. So they ordered the Hungarian soldiers to put me on a wagon and horse and buggy and took me in to a clinic.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I was waiting there in the clinic-- because there I had to wait until all the gentiles were taken care of, and at last I would be called in. While I was waiting there, a car pulled-- pulls up across the street, an open Mercedes, and some high-ranking German officers were getting off. But before they got off, there was a puddle of water-- it was after a rain-- so they yelled something, and, in a few minutes, a few people brought out a few Sefer Torahs, rolled them out, and the German, with his shining boots was walking out. I remembered-- that time came back to my mind, that picture, when the Torah fell out in the shul. And I thought that the sky will open, thunder and lightning will strike these guys because they were walking on the Sefer Torah. So at that point, I lost a little bit from my security of believing in--

speaker: interviewer
text: I just brought this up as an incident how people become face to face with, where is God? And is there any God? And if there is, why isn't he reacting? So anyhow, after the six months-- Wait. I have to find out about the pneumonia.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was healed from the pneumonia, except there is some-- --deposits, that I almost lost my ability to come to America, because they were afraid of TB, you know. But it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were treated. You know, I'm not clear on where you actually were. There was a city--

speaker: interviewee
text: In [NON-ENGLISH], they took me. We were far away from it when we were sleeping in our circles, but they took me into the city, where they gave me quinine-- quinine. This is-- before penicillin, this was the most effective, and that was unavailable for Jews, of course. And that took down the fever, and I got back to my work within a week or so, or less. So-- and this lasted until October, actually, 1944, when Szalasi, the Hungarian Nazi, came to power. You know the story that Horthy, the regent of Hungary, declared peace, and the same day the Hungarian Nazi party came to power. They deposed him, and then they took us over and deported us to Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who is that? The Germans, now?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. The Hungarians deported us, and the Germans took us from Austria on-- force-marched us to--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Mauthausen. This is October of 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We went through a ritual. At the Hungarian-Austro border, we had to resign from our Hungarian citizenship, and each one of us by name had to go over. One of them was-- his name was Kogan. So they beat him up. You son of a gun, you are related, probably, Kaganovich Kogan. He was a high-ranking Russian minister-- Stalin's brother-in-law, actually. The other guy's name was Joseph [? Karman. ?] [? Karman ?] is a Hungarian-- what can a Jew have such a good Hungarian name? They beat him up. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was that whole group of you that were in the forced labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were marched to the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marched to the border, and from the border, under Germans, marched to Mauthausen. On the way, whoever could not walk anymore was-- OK, sit down. And with us were Hungarian-speaking SS, because there was a group of Hungarians who joined the SS. They were in SS uniform. We did not know that they spoke Hungarian, and my uncle [? Naboch ?] was schlepping with him his tefillin. And he was, one morning--

speaker: interviewer
text: --davening there before we started. And this Hungarian SS noticed. Hey, what is this? So he took it off from his head, opened it with his side weapon, and there was, you know, in the tefillin, there is the Shema written in Hebrew. So he said, ah, you are a spy. You are carrying some secret message here. Started to be beat him, and it looked like he's going to beat him to death. But he fainted, so he lost interest in it. And we revived him and schlepped him along further.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyhow, whoever wasn't able to walk was shot on the side of the road. And we arrived to Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you started out? Do you have any idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because they threw us to another group. You know, they'd seemed to me that from-- as long as the eye could see, there were people marching on that road.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- did you have shoes and clothes of any kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: On that place where we were discharged from the Hungarians, I had on Hungarian army shoes. So they made me take it off, and they gave me some slippers. And I was walking on slippers until, a certain point, where one of the guys died. I took off his shoes, and then I had shoes on from the second or third day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it cold and raining already?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was slush. The snow was melting, because we were going through high mountains. We were going through the Alps-- [NON-ENGLISH], and so on. So anyhow, this is [INAUDIBLE] we arrived in Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you food on the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every once in a while. And when they gave, there was a rush for the food, and they gave a volley. They shot into the crowd and some people died, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived at Mauthausen. Your uncle was still alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. We arrived together. And we were there one-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --week, we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember-- [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --the registration, or how it-- There was no registration by then. It was a complete chaos. There was all the barracks, and even the tents were already occupied. We were sleeping outside, outdoors, on the stones, on the grass, wherever we could. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --certain days, they gathered a group of us and made us carry stones up the hill. And the second, another group was carrying the same stones down the hill. But this was a very tricky operation, because you went one by one uphill, and if somebody stumbled with a heavy stone, knocked down the guy like a domino. One knocked the other, and as soon as somebody fell down, the SS was there with their shepherds. And those were trained, and they went for the jugular, those dogs.

speaker: interviewer
text: The dogs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So they killed a lot of people in that manner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Mauthausen an extermination camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there selections every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- in those days already, it was a chaos. There was nothing done in the precise, organized German manner. There were some SS who, at their whim, did what they felt like. I remember one seemed to be a giant, a sergeant, who was going around, just walking among the people there who were sitting or lying down on the ground and pointing at one. And then with him were two other SS. They grabbed him, and they had a barrel full with water that they rolled on two wheels. And the two SS picked up that guy and pushed him head down into the water until he drowned. If he so happens [INAUDIBLE]-- and he had also a portable gallow with him, the same manner, a gallow that they pushed on two wheels. And sometimes he just grabbed two Jews and made them push the gallows, or the water. And even he made Jews pick up another Jew and--

speaker: interviewer
text: For no reason.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unbelievable, that guy was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there-- were there ovens there, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were two crematorias, but one of them was blown up by the time we went there. And the gas chamber, actually, didn't much work anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was a one-man band, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is the experience I had. I don't know, because there were, at that time, close to 20,000 people there. So who knew what-- we were only limited to a very small area, so I don't know what went on in the other areas, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about-- what was the tenor of what you'd talk about? I mean, when you were-- you were still--

speaker: interviewee
text: Food. Food. [LAUGHS] We were talking about if we were able to find, because by that time, we had lice that eating us up alive. And the lice was the one that carried the typhoid fever, also. And if-- and when we had strength, we were talking about food. And everybody was allowed one story, so mine was always-- mine was the Friday nights and the Friday night menu.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that? What was the menu?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gefilte fish, chicken soup with matzo ball, chicken paprikash-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --and cake, and the challah that my mother made. Everybody would talk about different meals they remembered?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did--

speaker: interviewee
text: We actually tormented each other-- [LAUGHTER] --because we were hungry, but it would be talking about food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you actually get to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We got bread, 12 of us, which was half of it sawdust, actually, [LAUGHS] we found out after, and one soup a day that was more liquid than anything else. And whenever the distribution of the food was, also, it was such a chaos, such a tumult everywhere that, many times, the push was so hard that we turned over the soup, and people were [LAUGHS] licking it up like pigs from the [? sty. ?] And it's unbelievable, what human beings can be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your uncle still kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not from Mauthausen, [INAUDIBLE]. And in Mauthausen, we met another uncle of mine who came back from Russia in time to be deported to [INAUDIBLE]. But he was still a strong, strapping six-footer, and he actually force-fed him. Force-fed the other uncle? Was that his brother? Yes. Yes. And that uncle also got liberated, and he lives now in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he's the one who actually saved my life, too, later on. So anyhow, we-- --lived like this for-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --four months in Mauthausen, and then the American armies-- --started to approach, and they evacuated Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is October of 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, it was until April, almost-- end of March or April. March.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's really longer. That's more like six months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Six months, yes. Six months. Time runs together. It's hard to tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever get inside to a barracks, or were you sleeping outside this whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were sleeping outside the whole time. And at one time, they were bombing Mauthausen. We thought, or the Germans said, that was the Americans. It is not true. They were dropping a few bombs for the world's consumption that the killing was done in the camps not by the Germans, but the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was actually the Germans dropping the bombs?

speaker: interviewee
text: They dropped three or four bombs, but nevertheless, in the middle of us-- and [LAUGHS] I remember it was at night or early morning. It was already getting a little light, and human parts were falling all over. So we just threw it away and continued to-- nothing, at that point, mattered anymore. From there, they evacuated us to Gunskirchen. Gunskirchen was a camp in the middle of the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who evacuated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: The German SS force-marched us, again, through mountains and on the roadside. Same story-- whoever could not walk was killed. Food we did not get. We were eating grass. And early in the morning, there were a lot of snails. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Snails.

speaker: interviewee
text: Snails, escargots. They did not discover. We discovered it much before they did. We ate them, and they were juicy, [LAUGHTER] delicious. The only problem was that we had to eat it with the house and everything, but that didn't matter either.

speaker: interviewer
text: You ate the shells?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Germans marched you from Mauthausen, did they clear out the whole camp? Did they march everybody from the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: One more episode in Mauthausen that I left out. This uncle of mine, whom I met up in Mauthausen, told me that my brother, my older brother, is in the tent in Mauthausen. I did not know then, but later I was told that those tents were actually made for the people who had the typhoid. And whoever went in there came out only through the crematoria. But I did not know it, so when they told me, pointed out which camp my brother was in, I climbed under, because the tents were fastened, and it was almost to the ground. I climbed in under it. And as soon as I got in, a Kapo caught me. You know what a Kapo is-- an inmate, whether a Jewish or criminal or whatever, but they were in charge. And they were the most ruthless killers. They [INAUDIBLE] They had to kill or else they were killed. So-- and he started to beat me, like, get the hell out of here, and so on. But he saved my life. Hadn't he chased me out, I would have never been able to get out. And my brother really was there. And you asked if everybody was evacuated, no. The tents were not evacuated. They were put on several boats on the Danube, because Mauthausen is near to the Danube. And they were to be sunk in the Danube. But the Americans arrived a little earlier than they thought. And many of those who survived the typhoid fever survived. And this brother of mine was the only one who survived the war. And he was the one who had the last chance, because he was in the tent. But this is, again, fate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, the Germans have taken you and your uncles, and they have marched you.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Gunskirchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that march take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Weeks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we may have left March. We arrived sometime in April, because there we were altogether one month, and we were liberated. In Gunskirchen, when we arrived, there were barracks that they were not completed. It had no floor. It was dirt, and it was in the springtime, so it was full with mud and water and whatnot. The windows had the frames, but no glass in it. [INAUDIBLE]. We arrived at night. They herded us into the barracks so tight that we could not sit down. We were standing only.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In Mauthausen, you know, the sonderkommando was the group who operated the crematorias. They were better fed, [BACKGROUND NOISE] but every month or six weeks, they were all thrown into the ovens and were killed, and a new group. But this very last group, they had no time to kill, so they came with us. And these kids were-- these were young, young Polish kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish Polish kids. And the group that I was in were several with us, and they were-- they had the [NON-ENGLISH], the strength, that they climbed up into the crossbeams and, with their belts, they tied themselves. And they were the only ones who were stretched out, comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the middle of the night, people were falling at each other, because they couldn't stand anymore, and there was a big tumult. So the Germans yelled in, quiet! Didn't help. They gave down a volley. A gunshot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they killed a bunch of people, naturally. And they shot one of the kids above my head. That night, all night long, the blood was dripping on my-- either my head or my shoulder or wherever. When the light came, my uncle looked at me, and I was covered with blood, so they thought that they shot me. So they started to strip me to see where the blood is coming from, but it was not. But I think that was the most horrible night of my life, like a Chinese torture. And I know it was blood, because I felt it was like glue sticking to me. And one more horrible experience was that every morning there-- we did not do a thing. And [INAUDIBLE] was the [INAUDIBLE] appell. That is when they chase everybody out to a huge area, and they count numbers. It was very advisable to get out as soon as one can from the barrack, because the last few dozen were kept behind, and they were dragging out the dead ones to the mass grave. And not only it was hard work to schlep them, to carry them to the grave and throw them in, but those playful SS, when they were in the mood, they kicked those in too who were throwing, and then-- so everybody tried to run out as fast as they can, could. And one morning I was running out as fast as I could, and a guy a few steps ahead of me fell-- fell into a little puddle of water, and was actually drowning in maybe five inches of water.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for that second, should I stop and try to help him? Because I thought I knew him. He was one of my original guys that I was together with. Or should I just take care of myself? And I did not stop. And because of it, for many, many years, I had nightmares seeing that scene, that that guy is drowning, you know, making gargling, in-- in that little water.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what is horrible-- and, again, we used to scream to God that how come that I am suffering from nightmares and these murderers living out long life, raising families, going to church and whatnot, and they have no conscience. So there is no such a thing, it seems like, the conscience, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyhow, on a Friday night, on May the 7th, I believe, we were liberated. I want to hear all about the liberation. Will you start with-- --how you knew that it was going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We started to hear a few days earlier, already, cannoning, canon fire. And the next day, we heard already the heavy weapons, like machine guns. And then after that we heard guns. We knew that something is going to happen. And one afternoon, the SS guard disappeared. When that happened, like a crazy hoard, there were still some 12-- more than 10,000 people, for sure, in that camp. And they all ran for the magazines, for the supply.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ammunition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the ammunition. Where the food was kept. And I was carried along, and actually, it was downstairs, the house. When they opened the door-- when the doors were already open, they swept me, and then all of a sudden, boom. I fell down, and a bunch of people on top of me. So at first, I thought I'd suffocate. And then, there, we found some bread, some mayonnaise and fat, pork fat, on top of it.
